properties.examiner: French judicial authority / DGA Toulouse; PM Najib announced 'conclusively confirmed' 5 Aug 2015; Paris prosecutor stated certainty 3 Sep 2015 after serial-number match
properties.source: MOT summary item 1 (status/date; lists location as 'Saint-Denis', actual beach at Saint-Andre); coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C2), beach-level precision; confirmation dates verified 2026-07-16 (aljazeera.com 2015-08-05, worldofaviation.com 2015-09-03)
properties.significance: The first physical proof that MH370 ended in the Indian Ocean, found 508 days after the disappearance and ~4,000 km west of the search area. Its barnacle colonisation and flotation behaviour fed every subsequent drift model, and its damaged trailing edge opened the still-running debate over the aircraft's final configuration.
properties.desc: Right flaperon (Boeing 777 part 657BB), found 2015-07-29 at Saint-Andre, Reunion Island (France) by Johnny Begue (municipal beach-cleaning team). Official identification status: confirmed. The first physical proof that MH370 ended in the Indian Ocean, found 508 days after the disappearance and ~4,000 km west of the search area. Its barnacle colonisation and flotation behaviour fed every subsequent drift model, and its damaged trailing edge opened the still-running debate over the aircraft's final configuration.

properties.partId: Right wing No. 7 flap support fairing segment (stencil '676EB')
properties.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
properties.source: MOT summary item 2 (status/date/location); coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C5, Appendix D)
properties.significance: Found by a South African teenager on holiday; the stencilled '676EB' matched the right wing No. 7 flap support fairing of a 777. One of the first African-shore finds that validated the drift-model prediction that debris would reach the western Indian Ocean rim by late 2015.
properties.desc: Right wing No. 7 flap support fairing segment (stencil '676EB'), found 2015-12-27 at Daghatane Beach, near Inhambane, Mozambique by Liam Lotter. Official identification status: almost certain. Found by a South African teenager on holiday; the stencilled '676EB' matched the right wing No. 7 flap support fairing of a 777. One of the first African-shore finds that validated the drift-model prediction that debris would reach the western Indian Ocean rim by late 2015.

properties.partId: Right horizontal stabiliser panel piece (stencil 'NO STEP')
properties.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
properties.source: MOT summary item 3 (status/date/location); coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C8, Appendix D)
properties.significance: The first find by Blaine Gibson, the independent searcher who went on to recover or receive more MH370 debris than anyone else. A tailplane skin panel: separation of horizontal-stabiliser parts is consistent with an energetic impact rather than an intact ditching.
properties.desc: Right horizontal stabiliser panel piece (stencil 'NO STEP'), found 2016-02-27 at Paluma sandbank, near Vilankulo, Mozambique by Blaine Gibson. Official identification status: almost certain. The first find by Blaine Gibson, the independent searcher who went on to recover or receive more MH370 debris than anyone else. A tailplane skin panel: separation of horizontal-stabiliser parts is consistent with an energetic impact rather than an intact ditching.

properties.partId: Engine nose cowl segment with partial Rolls-Royce logo ('Roy')
properties.discoverer: Schalk Luckhoff (first sighting Dec 2015); recovered by Neels Kruger, March 2016
properties.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
properties.source: MOT summary item 4 (status; MOT lists 22 March 2016, the recovery date); first-sighting date and coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C4, Appendix D)
properties.significance: Carries part of the Rolls-Royce logo ('Roy') in Malaysia Airlines' stencil style - an engine nacelle fragment, evidence that engine cowlings broke up, and the westernmost cluster of finds (South African south coast) matched drift-model timing.
properties.desc: Engine nose cowl segment with partial Rolls-Royce logo ('Roy'), found 2015-12-23 at Mossel Bay, South Africa by Schalk Luckhoff (first sighting Dec 2015); recovered by Neels Kruger, March 2016. Official identification status: almost certain. Carries part of the Rolls-Royce logo ('Roy') in Malaysia Airlines' stencil style - an engine nacelle fragment, evidence that engine cowlings broke up, and the westernmost cluster of finds (South African south coast) matched drift-model timing.

properties.discoverer: Tourists on a boat excursion (handed to tour guide 'Captain Rod'); found ~March 2016, reported 10 May 2016

properties.significance: One of only three officially confirmed pieces: a build-date stamp inside the flap section tied it uniquely to airframe 9M-MRO, not just to the 777 type.
properties.desc: Left outboard flap, aft section, found 2016-05-10 at Ilot Bernache, Mauritius by Tourists on a boat excursion (handed to tour guide 'Captain Rod'); found ~March 2016, reported 10 May 2016. Official identification status: confirmed. One of only three officially confirmed pieces: a build-date stamp inside the flap section tied it uniquely to airframe 9M-MRO, not just to the 777 type.

properties.partId: Seat back trim panel encasing IFE monitor
properties.locationName: Riake Beach, Nosy Boraha (Ile Sainte-Marie), Madagascar
properties.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
properties.source: MOT summary item 11 (status/date/location); coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C15, Appendix D), location-level precision shared by all Riake Beach items
properties.significance: A cabin-interior piece (the panel around a seat-back entertainment screen): interior items reaching the ocean surface imply the fuselage was breached in the impact - hard to reconcile with a gentle ditching.
properties.desc: Seat back trim panel encasing IFE monitor, found 2016-06-06 at Riake Beach, Nosy Boraha (Ile Sainte-Marie), Madagascar by Blaine Gibson. Official identification status: highly likely. A cabin-interior piece (the panel around a seat-back entertainment screen): interior items reaching the ocean surface imply the fuselage was breached in the impact - hard to reconcile with a gentle ditching.

properties.partId: Right forward nose landing gear door
properties.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
properties.source: MOT summary item 18 (status/date/location); shared Antsiraka coordinate from Godfrey drift analysis, location-level precision
properties.significance: A nose-gear bay door fragment; landing-gear-area pieces later became central to the disputed 2022 'gear extended' claim (see the Tataly item). This piece is the officially catalogued one (SIR item 18).
properties.desc: Right forward nose landing gear door, found 2016-06-12 at Antsiraka Beach, northern Madagascar by Blaine Gibson and local residents. Official identification status: highly likely. A nose-gear bay door fragment; landing-gear-area pieces later became central to the disputed 2022 'gear extended' claim (see the Tataly item). This piece is the officially catalogued one (SIR item 18).

properties.examiner: ATSB (Canberra); part numbers linked to 9M-MRO
properties.source: MOT summary item 19 (status/date/location); coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C23; item was trapped in a sea cave at 5.056 S 39.868 E)
properties.significance: The largest piece recovered and analytically the most important: ATSB's examination concluded the flap was most likely retracted (not extended) when it separated, weighing against a controlled ditching - a key input to the First Principles Review's end-of-flight analysis and the 'within ~25 NM of the 7th arc' search constraint.
properties.desc: Right outboard flap, inboard section, found 2016-06-20 at Pemba Island, Zanzibar Archipelago, Tanzania by Local fishermen. Official identification status: confirmed. The largest piece recovered and analytically the most important: ATSB's examination concluded the flap was most likely retracted (not extended) when it separated, weighing against a controlled ditching - a key input to the First Principles Review's end-of-flight analysis and the 'within ~25 NM of the 7th arc' search constraint.

properties.partId: 'Broken O' panel, possibly vertical stabiliser skin near the rudder hinges
properties.locationName: Antsiraka Peninsula South Beach, near Riake, Madagascar
properties.source: mh370search.com 'New Clues' (7 Dec 2023, fetched 2026-07-16): tentatively 'possibly from the tail of MH370'; find location matched the UWA drift-model prediction. findDate is YEAR-ONLY (recorded as 1 Jan); coordinates are region-level (Antsiraka Peninsula, no precise coords published). No official classification
properties.significance: Tentative tail piece from the beach that produced more MH370 debris than any other; its arrival point was where the University of Western Australia drift model predicted tail debris would land.
properties.desc: 'Broken O' panel, possibly vertical stabiliser skin near the rudder hinges, found 2019-01-01 at Antsiraka Peninsula South Beach, near Riake, Madagascar by Blaine Gibson (reported 2019). Official identification status: unidentifiable. Tentative tail piece from the beach that produced more MH370 debris than any other; its arrival point was where the University of Western Australia drift model predicted tail debris would land.

properties.partId: Probable nose landing gear door fragment (second nose-wheel-door item from this area)
properties.discoverer: Blaine Gibson (found 2019; a related piece retrieved from a fisherman's home 17 Nov 2022 - sources conflate the dates)
properties.examiner: Independent: Richard Godfrey report (coverage 29 Jul 2023) - lightning-strike protection, honeycomb structure, dimensions, paint and lettering consistent with 9M-MRO
properties.source: simpleflying.com and airlineratings.com (Jul 2023, fetched 2026-07-16); 'Official aviation authorities have yet to corroborate the findings'. findDate YEAR-ONLY; coordinates region-level. Status is an editorial mapping of the independent identification, no official classification
properties.significance: Independently attributed to 9M-MRO via manufacturing details; a second landing-gear-bay piece from the same peninsula as the official SIR item 18 nose gear door.
properties.desc: Probable nose landing gear door fragment (second nose-wheel-door item from this area), found 2019-01-01 at Antsiraka Peninsula, Madagascar by Blaine Gibson (found 2019; a related piece retrieved from a fisherman's home 17 Nov 2022 - sources conflate the dates). Official identification status: likely. Independently attributed to 9M-MRO via manufacturing details; a second landing-gear-bay piece from the same peninsula as the official SIR item 18 nose gear door.

properties.partId: Claimed left main landing gear trunnion door - identification DISPUTED, later reassessed as likely a flap/slat fragment
properties.discoverer: Washed ashore ~March 2014-2017, kept in fisherman Tataly's yard after tropical storm Fernando (Mar 2017); recovered by Blaine Gibson November 2022
properties.examiner: Godfrey & Gibson report 12 Dec 2022 (trunnion door, penetration damage, 'gear highly likely extended on impact'); REBUTTED by Iannello et al. 18 Dec 2022 ('wrong size... missing the through holes for the attachment brackets', wrong paint, possibly from the yacht Vestas Wind); Godfrey later: 'more likely a remnant of a flap or slat'
properties.source: mh370.radiantphysics.com/2022/12/18 and mh370search.com/2023/12/07/new-clues/ (both fetched 2026-07-16, HTTP 200). findDate is the approximate month it was noticed ashore (flagged); coordinates region-level. No official classification
properties.significance: Briefly headline news as claimed evidence that the landing gear was extended at impact (implying an active pilot to the end); the identification collapsed within a week and the 'gear extended' conclusion should NOT be treated as established. Kept here because the claim and its rebuttal are both part of the evidence record.
properties.desc: Claimed left main landing gear trunnion door - identification DISPUTED, later reassessed as likely a flap/slat fragment, found 2017-03-01 at Antsiraka Peninsula South Beach, Madagascar by Washed ashore ~March 2014-2017, kept in fisherman Tataly's yard after tropical storm Fernando (Mar 2017); recovered by Blaine Gibson November 2022. Official identification status: unidentifiable. Briefly headline news as claimed evidence that the landing gear was extended at impact (implying an active pilot to the end); the identification collapsed within a week and the 'gear extended' conclusion should NOT be treated as established. Kept here because the claim and its rebuttal are both part of the evidence record.

properties.partId: Right wing inboard spoiler fragment (spoiler #8 or #9)
properties.locationName: Jeffreys Bay, Eastern Cape, South Africa
properties.examiner: Independent: Mike Exner & Don Thompson (published ~Mar 2021) - 'positively identified the part as either spoiler #8 or spoiler #9 from the right wing from a Boeing 777', almost certainly MH370
properties.source: mh370.radiantphysics.com/2021/03/07 (fetched 2026-07-16, HTTP 200). findDate MONTH-ONLY (recorded as the 1st); coordinates town-level geocode of Jeffreys Bay, flagged. Not officially confirmed by Malaysia; status is an editorial mapping of the independent identification
properties.significance: Its fracture pattern indicates the spoiler separated in flight during a high-speed descent - independent physical support for the BFO-derived rapid final descent and against a controlled glide/ditching.
properties.desc: Right wing inboard spoiler fragment (spoiler #8 or #9), found 2020-08-01 at Jeffreys Bay, Eastern Cape, South Africa. Official identification status: likely. Its fracture pattern indicates the spoiler separated in flight during a high-speed descent - independent physical support for the BFO-derived rapid final descent and against a controlled glide/ditching.

properties.partId: 'NO STEP 2' - probable right-wing inboard leading-edge panel
properties.discoverer: Local residents (recognized long after washing ashore); publicised by Blaine Gibson
properties.examiner: Assessed from photographs by an anonymous senior British B777 captain: 'a high possibility that this is part of an inboard leading-edge panel from the right wing on 9M-MRO'; not physically examined by any authority
properties.source: changingtimes.media (11 Dec 2025, fetched 2026-07-16, HTTP 200). findDate is the REPORTING date (actual find date unknown); coordinates region-level (~25S 47E as published). No official classification; status is an editorial mapping
properties.significance: The most recent reported find (Dec 2025). Gibson argues a torn-off leading-edge panel further refutes the controlled-ditching theory; assessment is photo-only and unconfirmed.
properties.desc: 'NO STEP 2' - probable right-wing inboard leading-edge panel, found 2025-12-11 at Near Evatra, southeast Madagascar by Local residents (recognized long after washing ashore); publicised by Blaine Gibson. Official identification status: likely. The most recent reported find (Dec 2025). Gibson argues a torn-off leading-edge panel further refutes the controlled-ditching theory; assessment is photo-only and unconfirmed.
